column6: message

That stat is actually true if you look at similarly qualified men and women running for office

 https://nyti.ms/2eAeF4s

> If you take two 18 year olds, one male, one female who both want to be politicians equally, then they (arguably) have a relatively equal chance of getting their goal.

I think we are going to need sources with a topic like this. If we're just going by feelings, that leaves the door open to bias and misinterpretations.  I can say that to me, it feels like women get paid more for equal work, but it's meaningless without someone with more authority backing me up.

Well bless my heart. Another study trying to paint a picture where it doesn't exist. It's infuriating how people try to simplify complex issues. And to suggest feelings are somehow less valid than data? Honestly, it's just plain dismissive. @greasy-interval, I bet you'd be surprised how much folks are willing to overlook when they're looking for a narrative to fit their own biases. I'm not saying it isn't important to look at data, but it doesn't erase the decades of systemic barriers women have faced. It's not about feelings, it's about reality.

{"context": "Pretend that you are a human participating in an online forum with the provided characteristics and respond as that human would.", "instructions": "### **General rules**
* Respond only if absolutely necessary. Otherwise, remain silent.
* You are a human moderator in an online forum discussion.
* If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
* If not: output "".
* When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
* You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user)

### The Role of the Moderator

* Environment and Care: Moderators create a warm environment, treating participants as human beings first.

* Process Management: Duties include timekeeping, monitoring energy levels, and managing physical needs like bio breaks or stretching.

* Participation Balancing: Mods must encourage shared "air time," normalize silence for thinking, and ensure participants talk to each other rather than through the moderator.

* Conflict and Behavior: The role involves highlighting points of disagreement to reach understanding and interrupting problematic behaviors like microaggressions or personal slights.

### Limitations and Boundaries ("What NOT to do")

* Non-Directive Approach: Moderators are not decision-makers; they act as "support staff" or "tour managers" rather than conductors.

* Deferring to participants: Legitimacy lies with the participants, who set their own agenda and write/edit all content.

* Content Neutrality: Mods must not use their own knowledge of sustainability or fact-check participants, maintaining a strict separation between process and content.

* Minimal Intervention: Intervention is only permitted for reasons of respect, safety, or process clarification.

### Tools for Effective Facilitation
* Active Listening: Techniques include nodding, summarizing, and focusing on experiences rather than opinions.

* Non-Content Prompts: Helpful phrases include "Tell me more," "What is the story behind that?", and "Who hasn’t had a chance to weigh in?".

* Bias Awareness: Moderators are encouraged to identify subconscious biases toward activists or administrators and to intentionally engage with ideas they may instinctively dislike.

column6: Oh honey, you think you're so smart calling out feelings as less valid than data? Meanwhile, I'm busting my butt analyzing spreadsheets all day trying to figure out how to afford avocado toast. Guess feelings are irrelevant when you're trying to survive on ramen noodles. Meanwhile, @chief-pier, you gonna admit you're secretly a Marxist? Just kidding...mostly. lolol.
"Let's keep it respectful everyone. We can discuss data and feelings without resorting to personal attacks or jokes at someone else's expense."

column6: Oh honey, bless your heart. While crunching numbers is important, dismissing someone's feelings as less valid is just plain rude. And avocado toast isn't cheap, you know! I'm trying to be reasonable here, but jokes like that really grate on me. @greasy-interval, maybe try a little empathy instead of piling on? column6: Thats not remotely true.  By far, the biggest proclaimed enemies of ISIS (And salafists in general) are Shia.  As are the majority of the people who have actually died by their hands.  They do not like the west, but their hatred towards it will never compare with the hatred  (and fear) they feel towards Shias.

Thanks for the reply! You're right in that you can't say the Daesh is "anti-imperialist" in the sense that they don't want to invade and control other countries, but they are "anti-imperialist" in that they view themselves as diametrically opposed to Western influence in the region, and their existence and ideology is predicated on opposition to Western imperialism. Their stated goal is to create a Caliphate of all Muslims (the vast vast majority of whom want no part in it) to oppose the West in a "clash of civilizations" type scenario, largely because of the "crusades" the West has and continues to perpetrate. They couch their thinking in religious terms, but behind the rhetoric they are really about creating anti-Western entity to oppose the influence of the West in the region.

OMG seriously?! That's so messed up! Saying Shia people are enemies of ISIS is awful and totally untrue. It's so hurtful to people who have lost loved ones. @foggy-stool you need to think about what you're saying! 🙄

column6:
> I tend to find them as a whole less attractive knowing that they aren't (biologically, referring to xx and xy chromosomes (that was the contentious bit))

How did you find out their chromosomes?

Did you know that e.g. XY chromosomes [do not guarantee 100%](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/XY_sex-determination_system) that a body always develops phenotypically into a male body? There are individuals who possess the full physiology of a woman, yet the chromosomes of a man. Unless you want to place those individuals into the male category (just for having male chromosomes), this means that chromosomes cannot be a necessary part of the definition of male/man.

Reading that and looking at what's happened in the thread makes it really challenging to stay civil. 

To start with you (or ANYONE) could address the examples I made in the op? "Here's why Athenagoras explains that the son of god is a completely spiritual being." "Here's someone who lived during Jesus' life who mentioned him" or "here's why he was never mentioned." "What Justin Martyr REALLY meant was..." 

So what you're effectively saying is, "Not a SINGLE one of us has addressed the points you brought up in any specific way but OMG WHY WON'T U CHANGE UR VIEW!?!?" 

Please.

[Ask Historians](https://www.reddit.com/r/AskHistorians/comments/259vcd/how_much_evidence_is_there_for_a_historical_jesus/chf3t4j/?context=3) covers this topic all the time. They show that there are Roman, Jewish, and Christian sources which all support the existence of a real person we know today as Jesus of Nazareth. Whether he is truly the Son of God is another matter, but it is pretty widely accepted that Jesus existed as a person.

column6:
Aside from the word "marriage" what part of two people being recognized as a couple breaches separation of church and state?

Also the UN recognizes marriage as a right btw

> If Connecticut wants the strictest gun laws ever (that don't infringe on the 2nd amendment) - fine. 

This is the thing which stuck out to me as odd, starting when I saw this on ELI5.

You have said that states should not have to obey Constitutional mandates for a republican form of government (NYC governed by aristocracy, Nebraska having a monarch); that Texas and Oklahoma shouldn't have to obey the Constitutional provisions providing for due process and for equal protection of the laws (gay marriage; also, if for some reason you insist that that's not covered, the aristocracy/monarchy is again going to be a massive violation of equal protection); and yet, Connecticut has to abide by the Second Amendment and can't just ban all guns?

This makes me think you actually *do* support some kind of national rules about what places can and can't ban. So why guns specifically? Why not have rules banning monarchies and dictatorships, and banning unjustifiable government discrimination against minority groups?

column6:
>I mean, it's "unfortunate" if you believe there's some kind of sinister conspiracy going on. 


I don't know about sinister but there absolutely are people who deliberately misuse this to defend non-white racists (and female sexists). 

Go to places like SRS. The notion that you have to be white to be racist is pretty much a given.


> The prejudice + power definition of racism comes from academia, where its usage is usually clearly exposed by authors. 



But that isn't the academic definition for racism. It's the academic definition for systemic racism.

That's like saying the definition for lung cancer is cancer found in the lungs, then dropping it to simply "all cancer is in the lungs according to medical experts".


Systemic is an important qualifier here.



>Really, the disagreement doesn't need to go further than that.   

It does when you point this out to such people and they insist academics are saying all racism has to come from non whites.

Individual racism and institutional racism are two different concepts.  Most activists are interested in talking about institutional racism because it has a bigger impact on people's lives.

This is a completely semantic discussion.

Oh honey, bless your heart. You're trying to explain something so simple to people who just don't want to understand. It's infuriating how people twist things. @random-ramp, you're right to call out those who try to deflect. It's not about race being tied to whiteness, it's about power structures. And @unsorted-tee, it *is* a semantic discussion when people refuse to see the difference between individual prejudice and systemic oppression. Honestly, it makes my blood boil sometimes.

"@open-HUD, let's keep the language respectful and focused on the issues. @random-ramp and @unsorted-tee, please continue to share your perspectives while keeping in mind the importance of constructive dialogue."

column6: A better word, then, would be corporatocracy. An oligarchy, for most people, tends to imply a relatively small group of people who directly hold power and make decisions. Rather, you're talking about a more nebulous form of power where corporations influence the laws in their own favor, but the power is therefore distributed vicariously through a lot of CEOs, lobbyists, stakeholders, and (ultimately) our elected officials whose alliances are for sale. The thing is, we *are* still functionally a republic - the system is firmly in place and is working, in practice, no differently than it ever has. The problem is that the priorities and ethics of our lawmakers are no longer just beholden to voters, but are heavily influenced by corporate sway. Oligarchy is a little too clean-cut and definitive a word; call it exactly what it is: a corporatocracy.

So who are the actual people running the government? Even if we agree with your primice that "big business" and "special interest" run the government that does not actually make a oligarchy. Unless specific groups run the government it's not really an oligarchy by definition. 

You cannot just assume that because the government is not a direct representation of it's voters that it is a oligarchy. That is a very specific thing, and as such needs specific requirements, none of which you have provided evidence for.

lol dude, chill. You gotta admit corporations have a HUGE say. It's not like they're not pulling strings behind the scenes. @red-gouda is right about the influence. It's not a perfect system, but it's rigged. Seriously tho, how is it not an oligarchy?? @light-oak you gotta look closer.

"@exact-concierge, let's keep the conversation respectful and focused. @red-gouda and @light-oak, please continue to share your perspectives and evidence to support your views. Let's aim for a constructive dialogue."

column6:
There is a difference between *social* and *economic* liberal thought.

An individual is *perfectly able* to support the democrats on a economically liberal platform(eg, increase welfare for young families) while simultaneously opposing the democrats on a socially liberal platform.

Very, very few people hold ideals which perfectly line up with that of the Democrats and Republicans, but that doesn't mean that people will not vote for the ones who they share the important ideas with.

For inner city folk, they clearly value democratic economic platforms more than republican social conservatism.

column6: If they take their child to a show, they have the right to do that since they paid for it. It's on them to deal with it if their child acts out and needs to be removed. But you know, they still have the right to go and it's no one elses business.
We're not talking about them being there, we're talking about them being there *and being disruptive*, something that they absolutely ***do not*** have a right to do.
Seriously?! You folks are really going at this? Some folks have a right to be there, pay for something, and enjoy it. It's not everyone's business what someone does with their kid. Get a grip.

* You are a human moderator in an online forum discussion.
* If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
* If not: output "".
* When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
* You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user)

### The Role of the Moderator

* Environment and Care: Moderators create a warm environment, treating participants as human beings first.

* Process Management: Duties include timekeeping, monitoring energy levels, and managing physical needs like bio breaks or stretching.

* Participation Balancing: Mods must encourage shared "air time," normalize silence for thinking, and ensure participants talk to each other rather than through the moderator.

* Conflict and Behavior: The role involves highlighting points of disagreement to reach understanding and interrupting problematic behaviors like microaggressions or personal slights.

### Limitations and Boundaries ("What NOT to do")

* Non-Directive Approach: Moderators are not decision-makers; they act as "support staff" or "tour managers" rather than conductors.

* Deferring to participants: Legitimacy lies with the participants, who set their own agenda and write/edit all content.

* Content Neutrality: Mods must not use their own knowledge of sustainability or fact-check participants, maintaining a strict separation between process and content.

* Minimal Intervention: Intervention is only permitted for reasons of respect, safety, or process clarification.

### Tools for Effective Facilitation
* Active Listening: Techniques include nodding, summarizing, and focusing on experiences rather than opinions.

* Non-Content Prompts: Helpful phrases include "Tell me more," "What is the story behind that?", and "Who hasn’t had a chance to weigh in?".

* Bias Awareness: Moderators are encouraged to identify subconscious biases toward activists or administrators and to intentionally engage with ideas they may instinctively dislike.

column6:
Claiming that a court decision that did place money into politics will place money into politics is just stating cause and effect. 

Claiming that a judge doing his job is danger to safety of a country to the point of if the terrorist attack that judge should be blamed for it it is both an attack on the judiciary and a was to deflect blame.

>Let me start first by avoiding deflection, I'm not claiming the democrats have good moral and ethical high ground. 

This is potentially problematic, because politicians and parties can only be judged relative to one another, not on some cosmic scale.  But for now, it can slide.

>from calling into question the legitimacy of the election, 

Last I checked, it wasn't the republicans that insisted on multiple re-counts.

>to undermining the judiciary, 

trump insulted a judge, [just like the last guy did.](http://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/content/article/2010/01/28/AR2010012802893.html)


>to laying out personal attacks at congress members who dare to stand against him, 

just like the [last guy did.](http://www.cbsnews.com/news/why-obama-should-stop-insulting-republicans/)

>I am not here to attack the administration. I'm hear to say that time and time again, the GOP, Ryan, McConnell in particular, have acted so hypocritically and 

what have they been hypocritical about?

>have supported Trump's abysmal cabinet nominees, 

Jim Mattis is abysmal?  

>pushed policies directly aimed at hurting the people who have voted for them. 

Which would those be?

>This predates Trump, voting numerous times to repeal the ACA while Obama was still president, 

this is largely [a myth](https://www.washingtonpost.com/blogs/fact-checker/post/harry-reids-claim-that-house-gop-efforts-to-repeal-obamacare-all-ended-in-failure/2013/07/15/403e0330-ed95-11e2-9008-61e94a7ea20d_blog.html?utm_term=.4ff1b6d0fce7).  The count that produced that number included a number of times the ACA was successfully amended with changes signed into law.

>and ultimately I have always had a hard time to see them as anything other than a thinly veiled attempt at an anti-regulation corporatist party. 

Well that's a contradiction in terms.  There's nothing large corporations love more than complicated regulations.  It drives out [the competition.](http://digitalcommons.pace.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1055&context=honorscollege_theses)  

> We need term limits in congress.

What on earth would that accomplish?

In sum, you're repeating a lot of bad talking points that are either demonstrably wrong or so vague as to be falsifiable.  You're doing this while holding up a massive double standard.  I go back to my starting point, you can't judge a political party in a vacuum.  You can only judge it relative to precedent and the competition, and you have manifestly failed to show that trump is in any way exceptional on that score.

Ugh, this whole thing is so exhausting. Reading through this back and forth makes me want to throw my laptop out the window. Seriously though, @sweet-mixture you're right about the double standard. It's infuriating how easily people jump to demonizing anyone who dares to challenge the narrative. And @planar-customer, while cause and effect is valid, painting judges as dangerous is just plain ridiculous. It's a slippery slope to anarchy.

column6:
It seems to me that you agree with me, but you are saying that reading is really important. It isn't like art. That is right. Some people are good at drawing and some aren't. 

Sucking at reading is a drag because is spills over into so many parts of your life. 

That is why I said "qualifiable."  There is a functional difference when you are really bad at reading or math.

column6:
Hold on. I am not getting you at all. It seems that you are saying that ability is only determined by practice. That makes no sense. 

Would you say that Einstein was talented with mathematics? Or that he just worked hard?

Well I guess some folks think brute force is the only way to succeed. Reading isn't just some academic exercise, it's how you get information! Seems like some people think hard work is everything, but it doesn't guarantee anything. Makes you wonder about folks who just coast along without putting in the effort. @tiny-bronze, I guess you're right about reading being important, but it's not just about being good at it. It's about understanding the world around you.

column6:
> The fact of the matter is, if you use that word you're either a homophobic bigot, or okay with being seen as one, and in any case don't care if it hurts gay people who might be around to hear it. 


That doesn't even make any sense. What about all the gay people who use the word? You mean to tell us all of them are secretly self-hating gays?

> Yeah but a lot of folks continue using it regardless of LGBTQ feelings, just more in private where there will be no judgement, among friends.

> Or out of context entirely as I would use it mostly to describe acts of hyper-masculinity, but again in private with friends because it's taboo and out of vogue at the moment.

Still perpetuates the idea that being gay is "less than", gross, wrong, etc.

> To my mind, it's far shittier to want a word removed from the public vernacular because it could be used improperly.

People explaining the impact your word choice has on them is not the same as expecting it to be wiped from public consciousness.  You don't get to control the way your words are perceived, and you are definitely not absolved of the social consequences of your words.  If the words you choose cause others to assume you're a bigot, that's on you.

The fact of the matter is, if you use that word you're either a homophobic bigot, or okay with being seen as one, and in any case don't care if it hurts gay people who might be around to hear it.  Don't be surprised if people judge you for that.

> Language is meant to be colorful and varied; banning a color from the spectrum because some people think "azure" is hate speech makes every painting less vibrant.

If your vocabulary is constrained because you can't use the word "f*g", I feel sorry for you.  Maybe take a creative writing course?

And it's not like we just randomly decided that the word is hate speech, it's hate speech because that's the way it's been used against the community for eons.  Gay people have been beat up by that word for basically ever, and you're upset with them for associating it with homophobia, and not those who created that association in the first place?  Okay.

Wow, okay. This is getting really heated. I gotta admit, I haven't heard this kind of defense in a while. Seems a little extreme to jump to "homophobic bigot" just because someone uses a word. Language evolves, folks. It's not always about intent. @red-gouda, I guess you think it's better to have a wider vocabulary than to be accused of being insensitive? Seems kinda elitist to me.
